lessons.title: SIT Exam Format and Study Strategy
learning_objectives: Describe the structure of the NCEES Fundamentals of Surveying (FS) exam and how passing it leads to the Texas SIT credential.
List the major topic areas the FS exam covers and their approximate weighting.
Apply a high-yield study and test-taking strategy, including unit awareness and use of the reference handbook.
Identify common avoidable mistakes (unit slips, calculator mode, sign errors) and how to prevent them.
key_takeaways: Passing the NCEES Fundamentals of Surveying (FS) exam is the gateway to the Texas Surveyor-in-Training (SIT) credential.
The FS is computer-based, multiple-choice, closed-book except for the searchable NCEES FS Reference Handbook.
Heaviest topics are math/units, measurements/errors, distance/angles, leveling, traverse/coordinates, area, and GNSS.
Most lost points are avoidable: wrong calculator angle mode, unit mismatches, and sign errors on latitudes/departures.

content.html:
What this review is and how to use it

This module is a high-yield review, not a from-scratch course. It assumes you have already studied each topic and now want to refresh the formulas, work representative problems, and lock in the exam tips that win points. In Texas, the entry credential is the Surveyor-in-Training (SIT), and the gate to it is the NCEES Fundamentals of Surveying (FS) exam. Pass the FS, meet the education requirement, and TBPELS issues the SIT — the credential under which you accrue qualifying experience toward the RPLS.

The FS is a computer-based test (CBT) delivered at Pearson VUE centers. It is multiple-choice, with a fixed total of about 110 questions in roughly 5 hours 20 minutes (about 6 hours total with the tutorial and a scheduled break). It is closed-book except for the on-screen, searchable NCEES FS Reference Handbook — the only reference you may use. Knowing where formulas live in that handbook is itself an exam skill.

Topic areas and weighting

The FS exam blueprint spreads questions across the surveying fundamentals. Exact counts shift between specification updates, but the practical weighting for review purposes is stable: spend the most time where the most questions are. The computational core — mathematics, measurements and error theory, distance/angle measurement, leveling, traverse and coordinate geometry, and area — dominates the exam. Conceptual blocks — GNSS/positioning, geodesy/datums, photogrammetry/imaging, and professional practice/standards — round it out.

The remaining lessons in this module map onto those blocks. Treat each as a checklist: if you can work its problems quickly and explain its key formulas, you are ready for that slice of the exam. If you stall, that lesson flags where to drill.

content.text: Strategy: the FS rewards speed and accuracy on routine computations. You have roughly 2.9 minutes per question. Bank time on the easy quantitative items so you can afford the harder conceptual ones — and so you have margin to recheck units and signs.

content.html:
Test-taking strategy that scores

Three habits separate a comfortable pass from a near miss:

  • Read for the unit and the unknown first. Decide what is asked (degrees vs. radians, feet vs. meters, area vs. distance) before touching the calculator. Many distractor answers are the right number in the wrong unit.
  • Set your calculator deliberately. Confirm degree mode for survey angles; the single most common silent error is a calculator left in radians.
  • Estimate, then compute. A 10-second sanity estimate (the hypotenuse must exceed either leg; a bearing in the SE quadrant has an azimuth between 90° and 180°) catches gross errors and eliminates wrong choices.

Use the handbook search aggressively for the exact constant or formula form (e.g., the steel-tape coefficient, the average-end-area volume formula). Do not try to memorize what you can look up; do memorize the shape of each computation so you recognize it instantly.

content.text: Avoidable point-losers: (1) calculator in radian mode; (2) mixing feet and meters, or US survey foot vs. international foot; (3) sign errors on latitudes/departures (S and W are negative); (4) forgetting to halve in the shoelace area or in the curve tangent R·tan(I/2); (5) using a zenith angle as if it were a vertical (slope) angle.

content.title: Key Takeaways
items: The FS exam is the gateway to the Texas SIT; it is CBT, ~110 questions, ~5h20m, closed-book except the NCEES FS Reference Handbook.
Weight your review toward the computational core: math, measurements/errors, distance/angles, leveling, traverse, area.
Budget ~2.9 min/question; bank time on routine computations to fund harder conceptual ones.
Prevent the classic point-losers: calculator mode, unit mismatches, and sign errors.

flashcards.definition: The NCEES computer-based, multiple-choice exam that, with the education requirement, qualifies a candidate for the Surveyor-in-Training (SIT) credential; closed-book except for the searchable NCEES FS Reference Handbook.
flashcards.hint_1: It is the entry exam, not the PS exam.

flashcards.definition: The Texas entry-level surveying credential, earned by passing the FS exam and meeting the education requirement; the SIT accrues qualifying experience under an RPLS toward full licensure.
The only reference allowed during the FS exam — a searchable on-screen document containing the formulas, constants, and tables used on the test.

lessons.title: Mathematics Review for SIT
learning_objectives: Apply right-triangle trigonometry and the Pythagorean theorem to surveying geometry.
Use the law of sines and law of cosines for oblique triangles.
Convert fluently among degrees, radians, and DMS, and between common surveying units.
Compute distance and direction between two coordinate points (the inverse).
key_takeaways: Right triangle: c = sqrt(a^2 + b^2); SOH-CAH-TOA relates sides and angles.
Oblique triangles: law of sines a/sin A = b/sin B; law of cosines c^2 = a^2 + b^2 − 2ab·cos C.
1 ft = 0.3048 m (intl); 1 acre = 43,560 ft²; 1 deg = 60' = 3600"; radians = degrees × π/180.
Inverse between points: distance = hypot(ΔE, ΔN); azimuth = atan2(ΔE, ΔN) reduced to 0–360°.

content.html:
The trig you will actually use

Nearly every surveying computation reduces to a triangle. For a right triangle, the Pythagorean theorem and SOH-CAH-TOA cover almost everything: the hypotenuse is c = sqrt(a² + b²), and the three primary ratios relate an acute angle to the opposite, adjacent, and hypotenuse sides. On the FS you will use these to reduce slope distances, decompose a course into north-south and east-west components, and solve offsets.

The key habit is identifying which sides you know and choosing the ratio that involves your unknown. If you know the hypotenuse and need the side opposite an angle, use sine; adjacent, cosine; if you know both legs and need the angle, use the inverse tangent (arctangent). Keep the calculator in degree mode for survey angles.

Oblique triangles

When no angle is 90°, use the law of sines or law of cosines. Use the law of sines when you have an angle paired with its opposite side (ASA, AAS, or the ambiguous SSA); use the law of cosines when you have two sides and the included angle (SAS, to find the third side) or all three sides (SSS, to find an angle). These solve survey triangles such as a station occupied with two observed angles and one measured baseline.

Watch the SSA ambiguous case: the law of sines can yield two possible triangles. On the exam, the geometry of the problem (or a sketch) tells you which solution is physical — usually the one consistent with the stated configuration.

content.label: Law of sines and law of cosines

content.html:
Units, angles, and conversions

Surveyors juggle units constantly, and the FS punishes carelessness. Memorize the core conversions and confirm any constant in the handbook:

  • Length: 1 international foot = 0.3048 m exactly; 1 US survey foot = 1200/3937 m. The two differ by ~2 ppm — small per foot, but it matters over State Plane distances.
  • Area: 1 acre = 43,560 ft²; 1 acre = 10 square chains; 640 acres = 1 section (1 sq mile).
  • Angle: 1° = 60′ = 3600″; radians = degrees × π/180; a full circle = 360° = 2π rad. Some equipment reports in grads (gons): 400 grad = 360°.

To convert DMS to decimal degrees, add: deg + min/60 + sec/3600. To go back, take the whole degrees, multiply the fraction by 60 for minutes, and the leftover by 60 for seconds.

The inverse: distance and direction between two points

Given two coordinate points with northing (N, the y-axis) and easting (E, the x-axis), the inverse computation returns the straight-line distance and the azimuth between them. The distance is the hypotenuse of the coordinate differences, and the azimuth (clockwise from north) is the arctangent of the east difference over the north difference:

Crucially, use the two-argument arctangent, atan2(ΔE, ΔN), which places the result in the correct quadrant automatically; then add 360° if negative so the azimuth lies in 0–360°. A plain atan(ΔE/ΔN) loses the quadrant and is a frequent source of wrong answers.

content.latex: D=\sqrt{(\Delta E)^2+(\Delta N)^2}\qquad \text{Az}=\operatorname{atan2}(\Delta E,\ \Delta N)\ (\text{reduce to }0\text{–}360^\circ)
content.label: Inverse between two coordinates

content.text: Exam trap: keep your calculator in DEGREE mode for survey angles. When inverting coordinates, remember azimuth = atan2(ΔE, ΔN) — easting over northing — not the reverse, and reduce a negative result by adding 360°.

content.title: Key Takeaways
items: Master right-triangle trig (c = sqrt(a²+b²), SOH-CAH-TOA) and oblique-triangle laws (sines, cosines).
Know the conversions cold: 0.3048 m/ft, 43,560 ft²/acre, deg = rad × 180/π, DMS = d + m/60 + s/3600.
Inverse: D = hypot(ΔE, ΔN); azimuth = atan2(ΔE, ΔN), reduced to 0–360°.
Confirm degree mode and watch the SSA ambiguous case and the US-vs-international foot.

flashcards.definition: For any triangle, c² = a² + b² − 2ab·cos C — used to find the third side from two sides and the included angle (SAS), or an angle from three sides (SSS).
Computing the distance and azimuth between two known coordinate points: distance = hypot(ΔE, ΔN); azimuth = atan2(ΔE, ΔN) reduced to 0–360°.

flashcards.term: US survey foot vs. international foot
flashcards.definition: 1 international ft = 0.3048 m exactly; 1 US survey ft = 1200/3937 m. They differ by about 2 ppm — negligible for short distances but significant over State Plane coordinates.
The two-argument arctangent that returns an angle in the correct quadrant; in surveying, azimuth = atan2(ΔE, ΔN), avoiding the quadrant ambiguity of a single-argument arctangent.

lessons.title: Measurement and Error Theory Review
learning_objectives: Distinguish systematic errors, random errors, and mistakes (blunders), and the proper treatment of each.
Differentiate accuracy from precision and relate them to bias and scatter.
Compute the mean, residuals, standard deviation, and standard deviation of the mean for a set of observations.
Apply error propagation for sums and the rule for the error of a series and of the mean.
key_takeaways: Systematic errors follow a physical law and can be modeled/removed; random errors are unavoidable and follow the normal curve; mistakes must be found and discarded.
Accuracy = closeness to the true value (bias); precision = repeatability (scatter).
Standard deviation σ = sqrt(Σv²/(n−1)); standard deviation of the mean σ_m = σ/sqrt(n).
Error of a sum of independent quantities: E = sqrt(E1² + E2² + … ) (errors add in quadrature).

content.html:
Three kinds of error

Every measurement contains error; the skill is classifying it. Systematic errors follow a physical or instrumental law — tape too long, EDM scale error, a level out of adjustment. They accumulate in the same direction and can be modeled and removed by calibration or a correction formula. Random errors are the small, unavoidable fluctuations that remain after systematic effects are removed; they are equally likely to be plus or minus, follow the normal (Gaussian) distribution, and are handled statistically, never eliminated. Mistakes (blunders) — a transposed digit, a misread rod, the wrong target — are not errors in the statistical sense; they must be detected and discarded, typically through checks and redundancy.

On the FS, expect questions that ask you to classify a described error or to state how each type should be treated. The standard pairing: systematic → correct/calibrate; random → analyze statistically; blunder → find and remove.

content.text: Memory hook: Systematic = a Sign you can correct (one direction). Random = Roams both ways (normal curve). Mistake = Must be Removed.

content.html:
Accuracy vs. precision

These are not synonyms. Accuracy is how close a measurement is to the true value — it reflects bias (systematic error). Precision is how closely repeated measurements agree with each other — it reflects scatter (random error). The classic target analogy: tight grouping off-center is precise but inaccurate (a bias is present); a loose spread centered on the bullseye is accurate on average but imprecise. You want both: tight and centered.

This distinction drives practice. Repeating a measurement improves your knowledge of the mean (fights random error) but does nothing about a systematic bias — averaging a hundred reads of a mis-standardized tape still gives a biased answer. Bias is removed by calibration and corrections, not by repetition.

Basic statistics of observations

For n direct, equally weighted observations, the most probable value is the arithmetic mean. Each observation's residual is v = observation − mean. The spread is summarized by the sample standard deviation, using n−1 in the denominator (Bessel's correction) because one degree of freedom is consumed estimating the mean:

The standard deviation describes a single observation. The reliability of the mean itself improves with more observations: the standard deviation of the mean is σ divided by the square root of n. This is why redundant observations help — but only as sqrt(n), so the fourth observation helps far less than the second.

content.label: Mean, standard deviation, std. dev. of the mean

content.html:
Error propagation

When a result is computed from several measured quantities, their errors combine. For a sum or difference of independent quantities, the errors add in quadrature (root-sum-square): the total error is the square root of the sum of the squared component errors. This is the single most-tested propagation rule on the FS.

Two important special cases follow directly. The error of a series of n equal, independent measurements (e.g., a taped line made in n equal tape lengths each with error E) is E·sqrt(n). The error of the mean of n equal observations is E/sqrt(n) — the same sqrt(n) behavior we saw for the standard deviation of the mean. Recognize which case applies: accumulating along a line grows the error by sqrt(n); averaging shrinks it by sqrt(n).

content.text: Exam trap: errors add in quadrature (root-sum-square), not arithmetically. Adding errors linearly overstates the combined error. And remember n−1 (not n) in the sample standard deviation.

content.title: Key Takeaways
items: Classify error: systematic (correct/calibrate), random (analyze statistically), mistake (find and discard).
Accuracy = closeness to truth (bias); precision = repeatability (scatter); averaging fights random error, not bias.
σ = sqrt(Σv²/(n−1)); σ of the mean = σ/sqrt(n); 95% ≈ 1.96σ.
Errors of a sum combine in quadrature; a series grows as E·sqrt(n), a mean shrinks as E/sqrt(n).

flashcards.definition: Systematic errors follow a physical law, accumulate in one direction, and can be corrected/calibrated out; random errors are small unavoidable fluctuations of either sign that follow the normal distribution and are treated statistically.
Accuracy is closeness to the true value (reflects bias/systematic error); precision is repeatability/agreement of repeated measurements (reflects scatter/random error).
σ_m = σ/sqrt(n): the spread of the sample mean, which improves as the square root of the number of observations — the basis for taking redundant measurements.
For independent quantities added or subtracted, the combined error is the root-sum-square: E = sqrt(E1² + E2² + … ) — errors add in quadrature, not linearly.

lessons.title: Distance and Angle Measurement Review
learning_objectives: Reduce slope distance to horizontal using slope (vertical) angle or zenith angle.
Apply steel-tape corrections: temperature, tension/pull, sag, slope, and standardization.
Convert between quadrant bearings and azimuths and compute angles from directions.
Compute the angular misclosure of a closed traverse and the correction per angle.
key_takeaways: Horizontal distance: H = S·cos(vertical angle) = S·sin(zenith angle). Zenith is measured from vertical (90° = level).
Steel tape temperature correction: Ct = α(T − T0)L with α = 0.00000645/°F; slope correction ≈ −h²/(2L).
Azimuths run 0–360° clockwise from north; convert quadrant bearings by quadrant (NE: Az = β; SE: 180 − β; SW: 180 + β; NW: 360 − β).
Geometric check: interior angles of a closed polygon sum to (n − 2)·180°; misclosure ÷ n = correction per angle.

content.html:
Slope to horizontal reduction

Total stations and tapes measure along the line of sight (slope distance); boundary and mapping work needs horizontal distance. The reduction depends on which angle you have. With a vertical (slope) angle α measured up or down from horizontal, the horizontal distance is H = S·cosα. With a zenith angle z measured down from the vertical (so a level sight reads 90°), it is H = S·sin z. The two are complements: z + α = 90°.

This is a top exam trap. Confusing a zenith angle for a slope angle (using cosine when you should use sine, or vice versa) yields a plausible but wrong number. Read the problem: if a level shot would read 0°, it is a vertical angle (use cosine); if a level shot would read 90°, it is a zenith angle (use sine).

Steel-tape corrections

Although EDM dominates modern practice, the FS still tests taping corrections because they exercise systematic-error thinking. Each correction has a sign and a physical cause:

  • Temperature: Ct = α(T − T0)L, with the steel coefficient α = 0.00000645 per °F (T0 = 68°F standard). Hotter tape is longer → correction added.
  • Pull/tension: a tape pulled harder than standard stretches; Cp = (P − P0)L/(A·E).
  • Sag: an unsupported tape hangs in a catenary and reads too long; the sag correction is negative, −w²L³/(24P²) per span.
  • Slope: to reduce a slope-taped length to horizontal, subtract approximately h²/(2L), where h is the elevation difference over the length L.
  • Standardization: if the tape's true length differs from nominal, scale the measured distance by (actual/nominal).

Apply the right sign: corrections that make the line longer than measured are positive; those that shorten it are negative.

Bearings, azimuths, and angles from directions

Direction is expressed two ways. An azimuth is measured clockwise from north, 0° to 360° — a single number, easy to compute with. A quadrant bearing is an acute angle (0°–90°) measured from north or south toward east or west, e.g., N 40°15′ E or S 62° W. Convert by quadrant:

  • NE: Az = bearing angle β.
  • SE: Az = 180° − β.
  • SW: Az = 180° + β.
  • NW: Az = 360° − β.

To find the angle between two lines, subtract their azimuths (taking the absolute difference, and if it exceeds 180°, subtract from 360° for the interior angle). The back azimuth of a line is its forward azimuth ± 180°.

content.alt: Compass rose showing the four quadrants with the conversion rules between quadrant bearings and 0-360 degree azimuths
content.caption: Figure: Quadrant-to-azimuth conversion — NE: Az = β; SE: 180 − β; SW: 180 + β; NW: 360 − β.

content.html:
Angular misclosure of a closed traverse

A closed polygon traverse has a built-in geometric check: the sum of the interior angles must equal (n − 2) × 180°, where n is the number of sides/angles. The angular misclosure is the measured sum minus this theoretical sum. If it falls within the allowable tolerance (commonly a per-station limit times sqrt(n)), distribute it by applying an equal correction of misclosure ÷ n to each angle.

For an exterior-angle traverse the sum is (n + 2) × 180°; for a deflection-angle traverse the algebraic sum of deflection angles (right positive, left negative) is 360° for a closed loop. Know all three forms, but the interior-angle rule is the most common FS question.

content.text: Exam trap: zenith angle (from vertical, level = 90°) uses H = S·sin z; vertical/slope angle (from horizontal, level = 0°) uses H = S·cos α. The sag correction is always negative — an unsupported tape reads too long.

content.title: Key Takeaways
items: H = S·cos(slope angle) = S·sin(zenith angle); they are complements (z = 90° − α).
Tape corrections: temperature Ct = α(T−T0)L (α = 0.00000645/°F); sag and slope are always negative.
Quadrant↔azimuth by quadrant (NE: β; SE: 180−β; SW: 180+β; NW: 360−β); back azimuth = forward ± 180°.
Interior angles sum to (n−2)·180°; balance by spreading the misclosure equally (÷ n).

flashcards.definition: A zenith angle is measured down from the vertical (a level sight = 90°), so H = S·sin z; a vertical (slope) angle is measured from horizontal (level = 0°), so H = S·cos α. The two are complements.
Ct = α(T − T0)L, with α = 0.00000645/°F for steel and T0 = 68°F standard; a tape hotter than standard is longer, so the correction is positive (added).
NE: Az = β; SE: Az = 180° − β; SW: Az = 180° + β; NW: Az = 360° − β, where β is the bearing's acute angle. Azimuths run 0–360° clockwise from north.
For a closed-loop traverse, the interior angles must sum to (n − 2)·180°; the difference from the measured sum is the angular misclosure, distributed equally as misclosure ÷ n per angle.

lessons.title: Leveling Review
learning_objectives: Apply the height-of-instrument method: HI = BM + BS; elevation = HI − FS.
Distinguish backsight, foresight, turning point, and the difference between intermediate sights and FS to TPs.
Compute and check level-loop misclosure and apply allowable-error criteria.
Identify and eliminate systematic leveling errors by balancing backsight and foresight distances.
key_takeaways: HI = known elevation + backsight (BS, a 'plus' sight); new elevation = HI − foresight (FS, a 'minus' sight).
A turning point (TP) carries the line forward — it receives a FS then a BS; the rod must stay on the same point.
Arithmetic check: Σ backsights − Σ foresights = final elevation − starting elevation.
Balancing BS and FS distances cancels collimation, curvature, and refraction errors.

content.html:
Differential leveling: HI method

Differential leveling transfers elevation from a known benchmark to new points using a level and a graduated rod. The workhorse is the height-of-instrument (HI) method. You read a backsight (BS) on a point of known elevation; that read is a plus sight because it is added: HI = known elevation + BS. The HI is the elevation of the instrument's line of sight. To get a new point's elevation, you read a foresight (FS) there — a minus sight — and subtract: elevation = HI − FS.

Note the counterintuitive logic: a high rod reading means a low point (the line of sight hits higher up the rod). Backsights are added regardless of whether the point is uphill or downhill; foresights are subtracted. Keeping the plus/minus discipline straight is the whole game.

Turning points and intermediate sights

When the next point of interest is out of sight or too far, the surveyor sets a turning point (TP) — a stable, temporary point on which the rod stays fixed while the instrument moves ahead. At a TP you take a foresight (to compute its elevation) and then, after moving the level, a backsight (to re-establish HI). The rod must not move between the FS and the following BS or the line of levels is broken.

An intermediate sight (or side shot) is a foresight to a point whose elevation you want but which is not used to carry the line forward; it does not become a TP and is not part of the loop's BS/FS check. Only FS readings to TPs (and the final point) enter the arithmetic check below.

rows: Receives FS then BS to advance the line
Elevation only; not a TP, not in BS/FS check

content.html:
Loop closure and the arithmetic check

A leveling run should be checked two ways. The arithmetic (page) check verifies the bookkeeping: the sum of all backsights minus the sum of all foresights must equal the final elevation minus the starting elevation. If it does not, you made a math error in the notes.

The loop misclosure verifies the field work: when you level around a loop back to the starting benchmark (or close on a second known benchmark), the computed elevation should match the known value. The difference is the misclosure, which must fall within an allowable error — commonly expressed as a constant times sqrt(distance in miles or kilometers), e.g., 0.05·sqrt(M) ft for ordinary work. If within tolerance, the misclosure is distributed proportionally to the number of setups or the distance leveled.

content.label: Arithmetic check and allowable loop misclosure

content.html:
Systematic errors and how to cancel them

Leveling has three classic systematic errors that grow with sight distance: collimation (the line of sight not truly horizontal), earth curvature (the level surface drops away), and atmospheric refraction (the sight bends). The elegant field fix is to balance backsight and foresight distances at each setup: when BS and FS lengths are equal, these errors affect both readings by the same amount and cancel in the difference. This is the single most important leveling technique to state on the exam.

Curvature and refraction together are often combined as a correction of about 0.0206·K² feet (K in thousands of feet) or 0.0675·K² m (K in km), curvature lowering and refraction raising the apparent reading, with refraction roughly 1/7 of curvature. Other errors — rod not plumb, rod settlement at a TP — are minimized by good field procedure.

content.text: Exam trap: backsights are ALWAYS added and foresights ALWAYS subtracted, regardless of terrain — never key off whether the point is uphill or downhill. Balancing BS/FS distances cancels collimation, curvature, and refraction; an intermediate sight is not a turning point.

content.title: Key Takeaways
items: HI = known elevation + BS (plus sight); new elevation = HI − FS (minus sight).
A turning point receives a FS then a BS to carry the line forward; an intermediate sight does not.
Check: ΣBS − ΣFS = final − starting elevation; loop misclosure must be ≤ C·sqrt(distance).
Balance backsight and foresight distances to cancel collimation, curvature, and refraction.

flashcards.definition: The elevation of the level's line of sight: HI = (known point elevation) + backsight. New point elevations are HI − foresight.
A backsight (plus sight) is read on a point of known elevation and added to get HI; a foresight (minus sight) is read on a point of unknown elevation and subtracted from HI to get its elevation.
A stable temporary point on which the rod stays fixed while the instrument is moved ahead; it receives a foresight and then a backsight, carrying the line of levels forward.
Making backsight and foresight distances equal at each setup so collimation, earth-curvature, and refraction errors affect both readings equally and cancel in the elevation difference.

lessons.title: Coordinate Systems Review
learning_objectives: Distinguish horizontal and vertical datums (NAD83, NAVD88) and the geoid vs. the ellipsoid.
Explain State Plane Coordinate Systems, Lambert vs. Transverse Mercator projections, and the Texas zones.
Apply grid, ground, and combined scale factors to convert between grid and ground distances.
Relate geodetic latitude/longitude, ellipsoid/orthometric/geoid heights, and convergence (mapping angle).
key_takeaways: NAD83 is the horizontal datum (on the GRS80 ellipsoid); NAVD88 is the vertical datum (orthometric heights from the geoid).
Orthometric height H = ellipsoid height h − geoid height N (H = h − N).
Texas SPCS uses the Lambert Conformal Conic projection with five zones (N, NC, C, SC, S).
Combined factor = grid scale factor × elevation (sea-level) factor; ground distance = grid distance ÷ combined factor.

content.html:
Datums: horizontal and vertical

A datum is the reference framework that gives coordinates meaning. The modern horizontal datum in the U.S. is NAD83, realized on the GRS80 ellipsoid — a smooth mathematical surface approximating the Earth, on which geodetic latitude and longitude are defined. The modern vertical datum is NAVD88, which expresses orthometric heights (elevations) referenced to the geoid, the equipotential surface approximating mean sea level.

The geoid and the ellipsoid are different surfaces. GNSS measures height above the ellipsoid (ellipsoid height, h), but engineering and mapping need height above the geoid (orthometric height, H, which is what water levels respond to). They differ by the geoid height N: H = h − N. In the conterminous U.S., N is negative (the geoid is below the ellipsoid), so orthometric heights are larger than ellipsoid heights. NGS geoid models (e.g., GEOID18) supply N.

content.text: Memory hook: H = h − N. Orthometric height (H, what you survey to) equals GNSS ellipsoid height (h) minus the geoid undulation (N). In the U.S., N is negative, so H comes out larger than h.

content.html:
State Plane Coordinate Systems

The State Plane Coordinate System (SPCS) maps the curved Earth onto a flat grid so surveyors can use plane coordinates (northing, easting) over a state-sized area while keeping distortion small (designed to about 1 part in 10,000). Two conformal projections are used: the Lambert Conformal Conic for zones that are wide east-west, and the Transverse Mercator for zones that are tall north-south. A state is divided into zones to bound the distortion.

Texas uses the Lambert Conformal Conic projection (because the state is wider than it is tall) with five zones: North, North Central, Central, South Central, and South. Each zone has its own defining parallels and false origin. A surveyor working near a county line must confirm which zone applies, because coordinates are zone-specific.

content.text: Texas specifics: Texas SPCS uses the Lambert Conformal Conic projection in five zones (North, North Central, Central, South Central, South). A project straddling a zone boundary must state which zone its coordinates are in; values are not interchangeable between zones.

content.html:
Grid, ground, and the combined factor

SPCS distances are grid distances, not the distances you measure on the ground. Two effects separate them. The grid scale factor (k) accounts for the projection (the grid is slightly short along the standard parallels and slightly long away from them). The elevation (sea-level) factor reduces a ground distance down to the ellipsoid, since a line at elevation is longer than its projection on the ellipsoid: elevation factor ≈ R/(R + h), where R is the mean Earth radius (~20,906,000 ft).

Multiply them to get the combined factor. Then: grid distance = ground distance × combined factor, and inversely ground distance = grid distance ÷ combined factor. Mixing these up — or applying the factor in the wrong direction — is a frequent error. As a rule, ground distances are usually a little longer than grid distances.

rows: Horizontal datum on the GRS80 ellipsoid; basis of geodetic lat/long and SPCS
Separation between geoid and ellipsoid; H = h − N
Projection distortion of length at a point
R/(R+h); reduces ground distance to the ellipsoid
Angle between grid north and geodetic north; azimuth_grid = azimuth_geodetic − γ

content.html:
North directions and convergence

On a projection, grid north (the y-axis) and geodetic (true) north generally do not coincide except along the central meridian. Their difference is the convergence angle (or mapping angle), γ. To convert a geodetic azimuth to a grid azimuth you apply convergence, and a small arc-to-chord (t−T) correction may apply for long lines. For most FS questions, remember the relationship grid azimuth = geodetic azimuth − convergence (with the sign depending on which side of the central meridian you are on), and keep three norths straight: magnetic, true/geodetic, and grid.

content.title: Key Takeaways
items: NAD83 (GRS80 ellipsoid) is the horizontal datum; NAVD88 gives orthometric heights from the geoid.
H = h − N: orthometric = ellipsoid height − geoid height (N is negative in the U.S.).
Texas SPCS = Lambert Conformal Conic in five zones (N, NC, C, SC, S).
Combined factor = grid scale × elevation factor; ground = grid ÷ combined factor.

flashcards.definition: NAD83 is the horizontal datum (geodetic positions on the GRS80 ellipsoid); NAVD88 is the vertical datum (orthometric heights referenced to the geoid).
N is the separation between the geoid and the ellipsoid; orthometric height H equals GNSS ellipsoid height h minus N. In the conterminous U.S. N is negative, so H > h.
The Texas SPCS uses the Lambert Conformal Conic projection divided into five zones — North, North Central, Central, South Central, and South — each with its own defining parallels.
The product of the grid scale factor and the elevation (sea-level) factor; grid distance = ground distance × combined factor, and ground distance = grid distance ÷ combined factor.

lessons.title: Traverse Computations Review
learning_objectives: Compute latitudes and departures of traverse courses from bearings/azimuths and distances.
Determine the linear misclosure and relative precision (ratio of closure) of a traverse.
Adjust a traverse by the compass (Bowditch) rule and compute adjusted coordinates.
Apply the correct signs to latitudes and departures by quadrant.
key_takeaways: Latitude = distance·cos(azimuth); departure = distance·sin(azimuth). North/East positive; South/West negative.
Linear misclosure = sqrt((Σlat)² + (Σdep)²); for a closed loop the sums should be zero.
Relative precision = misclosure : perimeter, expressed as 1 : (perimeter ÷ misclosure).
Compass (Bowditch) rule: distribute misclosure to each course in proportion to its length.

content.html:
Latitudes and departures

A traverse is a connected series of courses, each with a direction and a distance. To compute coordinates, decompose every course into a north-south component (latitude) and an east-west component (departure). Using azimuth (clockwise from north): latitude = distance · cos(azimuth) and departure = distance · sin(azimuth). The trig functions deliver the signs automatically when you use azimuths; with quadrant bearings you must assign the sign by quadrant.

Sign convention is the heart of the computation: north and east are positive; south and west are negative. A course running S W has a negative latitude and a negative departure. Getting a single sign wrong throws off every downstream coordinate, so verify each course's quadrant against its computed signs.

content.label: Latitude and departure of a course

content.html:
Misclosure and relative precision

For a closed-loop traverse that returns to its start, the latitudes should sum to zero and the departures should sum to zero. They never quite do; the residual sums are the misclosure in latitude and misclosure in departure. The linear misclosure is their hypotenuse: sqrt((Σlat)² + (Σdep)²). (For a closed connecting traverse between two known points, compare the summed latitudes/departures to the known coordinate differences.)

The quality of the work is judged by relative precision (ratio of closure): the linear misclosure divided into the total perimeter, expressed as a ratio 1 : (perimeter ÷ misclosure). A result of 1:12,000 means the misclosure is one part in twelve thousand of the distance traversed. Compare against the standard required for the survey class (e.g., 1:5,000 for many boundary surveys, higher for control).

Adjusting with the compass (Bowditch) rule

Once the misclosure is within tolerance, distribute it so the traverse closes perfectly. The most common method is the compass rule (Bowditch rule), which assumes the precision of angles and distances is balanced and therefore distributes the misclosure to each course in proportion to that course's length. For each course, the correction to its latitude is

correction to latitude = −(Σlat) × (course length ÷ perimeter)

with the analogous expression for departure using Σdep. The negative sign removes the accumulated misclosure. Apply the corrections, then accumulate the adjusted latitudes and departures from the starting coordinate to obtain the adjusted coordinates of each station. (The transit rule distributes in proportion to the latitude/departure magnitude instead; the least-squares adjustment is the rigorous method.)

content.alt: Closed-loop traverse polygon with each course decomposed into latitude and departure, and the misclosure vector at the closing point
content.caption: Figure: A closed traverse — each course resolved into latitude (N-S) and departure (E-W), with the small misclosure vector closing the loop.

content.text: Exam trap: assign latitude/departure signs by quadrant (S and W are negative). Relative precision is perimeter ÷ misclosure, reported as 1:N — a larger N is better. The compass-rule correction is proportional to course length, the transit rule to lat/dep magnitude.

content.title: Key Takeaways
items: Lat = D·cos(Az), Dep = D·sin(Az); north/east +, south/west −.
Linear misclosure = hypot(Σlat, Σdep); loop sums should be zero.
Relative precision = 1 : (perimeter ÷ misclosure); bigger denominator = better.
Compass (Bowditch) rule spreads misclosure proportionally to course length to get adjusted coordinates.

flashcards.definition: The north-south (latitude = D·cos Az) and east-west (departure = D·sin Az) components of a traverse course; north/east are positive, south/west negative.
The closure error of a traverse: sqrt((Σlatitudes)² + (Σdepartures)²). For a closed loop the latitude and departure sums should each be zero.
The traverse misclosure expressed as a fraction of the perimeter: 1 : (perimeter ÷ misclosure). A larger denominator (e.g., 1:15,000 vs. 1:5,000) indicates better work.
A traverse adjustment that distributes the latitude and departure misclosure to each course in proportion to the course's length, assuming balanced angular and linear precision.

lessons.title: Area and Volume Review
learning_objectives: Compute parcel area from coordinates using the shoelace (coordinate) method.
Compute area by the double-meridian-distance (DMD) method using latitudes and departures.
Estimate irregular areas by the trapezoidal rule and Simpson's one-third rule.
Compute earthwork volumes by the average-end-area and prismoidal methods.
key_takeaways: Shoelace area: A = ½·|Σ(x_i·y_{i+1} − x_{i+1}·y_i)|; convert ft² to acres by ÷ 43,560.
DMD area = ½·|Σ(DMD × latitude)|; DMD of a course = previous DMD + previous departure + present departure.
Trapezoidal rule: A = d·[(h0 + hn)/2 + (h1 + … + h_{n−1})]; Simpson's ⅓ needs an even number of intervals.
Average-end-area volume V = L·(A1 + A2)/2; divide ft³ by 27 for cubic yards.

content.html:
Area by coordinates (the shoelace method)

When the corners of a parcel are known as coordinates, the shoelace (coordinate) method gives the area directly. Going around the polygon in order, cross-multiply each point's coordinates with the next point's, sum the differences, take the absolute value, and halve it:

The name comes from the criss-cross pattern of the multiplications. Take the absolute value (a clockwise traverse yields a negative raw sum) and remember the factor of one-half — forgetting it doubles the area. To report in acres, divide square feet by 43,560.

Area by double meridian distance (DMD)

Before coordinates were universal, area was computed from latitudes and departures using the double-meridian-distance (DMD) method — still tested and still handy directly off an adjusted traverse. The DMD of a course equals the DMD of the previous course plus the departure of the previous course plus the departure of the present course. The first course's DMD equals its own departure. Then the double area is the sum of (DMD × latitude) for all courses, and the area is half its absolute value.

The method is just the shoelace formula reorganized around a reference meridian, which is why both give the same answer. Use whichever the problem hands you: coordinates → shoelace; latitudes/departures → DMD.

Irregular areas: trapezoidal and Simpson's rules

For an area bounded by an irregular line (a stream, a road edge) sampled by offsets at equal spacing d, two numerical rules apply. The trapezoidal rule treats each strip as a trapezoid: A = d·[(h₀ + hₙ)/2 + (h₁ + h₂ + … + hₙ₋₁)] — half the two end offsets plus all the interior offsets, times the spacing.

Simpson's one-third rule fits parabolas through groups of points and is more accurate for curved boundaries, but it requires an even number of intervals (odd number of offsets): A = (d/3)·[h₀ + hₙ + 4(odd-indexed offsets) + 2(even-indexed interior offsets)]. If you have an odd number of intervals, handle the last strip separately with the trapezoidal rule.

Volumes: average end area and prismoidal

Earthwork volume between two cross-sections a distance L apart is estimated by the average-end-area method: V = L · (A₁ + A₂)/2, where A₁ and A₂ are the cross-sectional areas at the ends. It is simple and slightly overestimates volume for sections that taper. Divide cubic feet by 27 to get cubic yards, the standard earthwork unit.

The prismoidal formula is more accurate: V = (L/6)(A₁ + 4Aₘ + A₂), where Aₘ is the area of the middle section (computed from averaged dimensions, not the average of the two end areas). The difference between the two methods is the prismoidal correction. For exam purposes: average-end-area is the default; reach for the prismoidal formula when the problem stresses accuracy or supplies a middle section.

content.text: Exam trap: don't forget the ½ in the shoelace/DMD area, take the absolute value, and convert ft²→acres by ÷43,560. Simpson's ⅓ rule needs an EVEN number of intervals. Convert ft³→yd³ by ÷27, not ÷9.

content.title: Key Takeaways
items: Shoelace area A = ½|Σ(x_i y_{i+1} − x_{i+1} y_i)|; ÷43,560 for acres.
DMD area = ½|Σ(DMD·Lat)|; DMD_i = DMD_{i−1} + Dep_{i−1} + Dep_i.
Trapezoidal rule for any offset count; Simpson's ⅓ needs an even number of intervals.
Volume: average end area V = L(A1+A2)/2 (÷27 for cubic yards); prismoidal is more accurate.

flashcards.definition: A = ½·|Σ(x_i·y_{i+1} − x_{i+1}·y_i)| computed around the polygon; take the absolute value and divide ft² by 43,560 to get acres.
A course's DMD = previous DMD + previous departure + present departure (first = its own departure); area = ½|Σ(DMD × latitude)|.
A numerical area rule, A = (d/3)[ends + 4·(odd offsets) + 2·(even interior offsets)], that fits parabolas and requires an even number of equal intervals.
V = L·(A1 + A2)/2 — the volume of earthwork between two cross-sections of areas A1 and A2 a distance L apart; divide ft³ by 27 for cubic yards.

lessons.title: GPS/GNSS Concepts Review
learning_objectives: Explain GNSS positioning by trilateration and the role of the receiver-clock bias (four-satellite minimum).
Distinguish code-pseudorange and carrier-phase observations and their accuracy levels.
Compare positioning methods: autonomous, DGPS, static, RTK, and network RTK.
Describe error sources and the effect of satellite geometry (DOP) on positioning accuracy.
key_takeaways: GNSS positions by trilateration from satellite ranges; a 4th satellite solves the receiver clock bias, so 4 are needed for 3D.
Carrier-phase (cm-level, needs ambiguity resolution) is far more precise than code pseudorange (m-level).
Static/post-processed gives the highest accuracy; RTK gives real-time cm accuracy via a base/correction stream.
Lower DOP (well-spread satellites) means better accuracy; major errors are ionosphere, troposphere, orbit/clock, and multipath.

content.html:
How GNSS computes a position

GNSS (the umbrella term for GPS, GLONASS, Galileo, BeiDou) positions a receiver by trilateration: each satellite broadcasts its position and time, the receiver measures the travel time of the signal to compute a range, and the intersection of ranges from several satellites fixes the receiver's location. Because the receiver's inexpensive clock is not perfectly synchronized with the satellites' atomic clocks, every measured range contains a common clock-bias error — hence the term pseudorange.

That clock bias is a fourth unknown alongside the three position coordinates (X, Y, Z). Therefore a 3-D fix requires a minimum of four satellites: three for position and one to solve the receiver-clock offset. More satellites add redundancy and improve the solution.

content.text: Why four satellites? Three unknowns of position (X, Y, Z) plus one receiver-clock bias = four unknowns, so four pseudorange equations are the minimum for a 3-D position fix.

content.html:
Code vs. carrier-phase observations

Receivers can measure range two ways. The code (pseudorange) observable matches the satellite's broadcast code to a receiver-generated replica; it is robust and instantaneous but only meter-level accurate — fine for navigation, not for surveying. The carrier-phase observable measures the phase of the underlying carrier wave (wavelength ~19–24 cm), which is far finer — centimeter to millimeter level — but introduces the integer ambiguity: the receiver knows the fractional phase but not the whole number of cycles to the satellite. Surveying-grade positioning depends on resolving that ambiguity (“fixing” the solution).

Carrier-phase processing is what makes GNSS a surveying tool. A “float” solution (ambiguity unresolved) is decimeter-level; a “fixed” solution (ambiguity resolved) is centimeter-level. Most precision methods — static and RTK alike — rely on carrier phase and differencing between receivers to cancel common errors.

Positioning methods compared

The methods trade accuracy against speed and convenience:

  • Autonomous: a single receiver using code; a few meters — navigation only.
  • DGPS/SBAS: code with differential corrections; sub-meter — GIS and mapping.
  • RTK (real-time kinematic): carrier phase with a base station (or a network RTK/RTN service) streaming corrections to the rover; real-time centimeter accuracy — the workhorse of field surveying.
  • Static: long simultaneous occupations on two or more points, carrier-phase data post-processed; the highest accuracy — control and geodetic work. (Rapid-static and PPP are variants.)

Choose by the job: control points and long baselines favor static; production topographic and stakeout work favor RTK; reconnaissance and GIS favor DGPS.

Error sources and satellite geometry (DOP)

GNSS accuracy is limited by predictable error sources: ionospheric and tropospheric delay (the atmosphere slows the signal), satellite orbit and clock errors, and multipath (signals reflecting off nearby surfaces before reaching the antenna). Differential techniques cancel the errors common to two nearby receivers (atmosphere, orbit, satellite clock); multipath is site-specific and is fought with good antennas and clear sky.

Beyond the ranging errors, the geometry of the visible satellites matters. Dilution of precision (DOP) quantifies how satellite spread amplifies range error into position error. Satellites bunched together give a high DOP (poor geometry, larger error); satellites well-distributed across the sky give a low DOP (better). A low PDOP/GDOP is a precondition for a reliable fix — the same ranging precision yields a much better position when the geometry is strong.

content.text: Exam trap: a LOWER DOP is better (good geometry). Four satellites is the minimum for 3-D because the receiver clock bias is a fourth unknown. Code = meter-level; carrier phase (ambiguity resolved) = centimeter-level.

content.title: Key Takeaways
items: GNSS trilaterates from pseudoranges; 4 satellites minimum (3 position unknowns + receiver clock bias).
Code is meter-level; carrier phase with resolved integer ambiguity is centimeter-level.
Static (post-processed) = highest accuracy; RTK/network RTK = real-time centimeter; DGPS = sub-meter.
Errors: ionosphere, troposphere, orbit/clock, multipath; lower DOP (better geometry) = better accuracy.

flashcards.definition: A 3-D GNSS fix has four unknowns — three position coordinates plus the receiver-clock bias — so a minimum of four satellite pseudorange measurements is required.

Code (pseudorange) positioning is meter-level and instantaneous; carrier-phase positioning is centimeter-level but requires resolving the integer ambiguity (the unknown whole number of carrier cycles).

RTK gives real-time centimeter accuracy using a base/network correction stream; static occupies points for an extended time and post-processes carrier-phase data for the highest accuracy (control/geodetic work).
A measure of how satellite geometry amplifies ranging error into position error; well-spread satellites give a low DOP (good), bunched satellites a high DOP (poor).

lessons.title: SIT Practice Problems — Set 1
learning_objectives: Solve mixed, exam-style fundamentals problems under realistic time pressure.
Select the correct formula and units for distance, angle, traverse, area, and curve problems.
Self-check answers with estimates and unit reasoning.
Identify and correct the common point-losing mistakes flagged throughout this module.
key_takeaways: Work each problem by: identify the unknown and unit → pick the formula → check signs/quadrant → estimate → compute.
Carry a degree-mode calculator and watch zenith-vs-vertical angles and lat/dep signs.
For area, never forget the ½ and the ÷43,560; for curves, the tangent is R·tan(I/2).
Use the randomized problem templates in this module to drill speed and accuracy before exam day.

content.html:
How to use this problem set

This final lesson is practice, not new theory. The accompanying randomized problem templates regenerate fresh numbers each attempt, spanning the computational core of the FS: right-triangle/Pythagorean geometry, bearing-to-azimuth conversion, latitude/departure, traverse misclosure, area by coordinates, an EDM/temperature correction, and a horizontal-curve tangent. Work them repeatedly until each becomes automatic.

Adopt a consistent five-step routine for every problem: (1) identify the unknown and its unit; (2) choose the formula; (3) check signs, quadrant, and angle mode; (4) estimate the magnitude; (5) compute and convert. The estimate step alone catches most blunders — if your hypotenuse is shorter than a leg, or your traverse misclosure is larger than a course, stop and recheck.

sections.title: Worked example 1 — Slope to horizontal
sections.content:
A total station reads a slope distance of 642.18 ft at a zenith angle of 86°30′. Find the horizontal distance.

Solution. Zenith angle → use H = S·sin z. Convert 86°30′ = 86.5°. sin(86.5°) = 0.99813. H = 642.18 × 0.99813 = 641.0 ft. Check: the sight is nearly level, so H should be just under S — it is.

sections.title: Worked example 2 — Bearing to azimuth
sections.content:
Convert the bearing S 47°15′ W to an azimuth.

Solution. SW quadrant → Az = 180° + bearing angle. 47°15′ = 47.25°. Az = 180° + 47.25° = 227.25° (227°15′). Check: a southwest line must have an azimuth between 180° and 270° — it does.

sections.title: Worked example 3 — Traverse misclosure & precision
sections.content:
A closed traverse with a 2,480-ft perimeter has Σlatitudes = +0.06 ft and Σdepartures = −0.08 ft. Find the linear misclosure and relative precision.

Solution. E = sqrt(0.06² + 0.08²) = sqrt(0.0036 + 0.0064) = sqrt(0.01) = 0.10 ft. Precision = 2,480 ÷ 0.10 = 24,800, i.e., 1:24,800 — well within typical boundary tolerance.

sections.title: Worked example 4 — Area by coordinates
sections.content:
A triangle has corners A(0,0), B(400,0), C(400,300) ft. Find the area in acres.

Solution. Shoelace: 2A = |x_A(y_B−y_C) + x_B(y_C−y_A) + x_C(y_A−y_B)| = |0(0−300) + 400(300−0) + 400(0−0)| = 120,000. A = 60,000 ft². In acres: 60,000 ÷ 43,560 = 1.377 ac.

sections.title: Worked example 5 — Horizontal curve tangent
sections.content:
A simple curve has R = 800 ft and intersection angle I = 36°. Find the tangent length T.

Solution. T = R·tan(I/2) = 800 × tan(18°) = 800 × 0.32492 = 259.9 ft. Don't forget to halve I before taking the tangent.

content.html:
Pace and self-check

On the real exam you have roughly 2.9 minutes per question. Practice to a comfortable margin below that so you can afford to recheck. When you finish a computation, run the three fastest checks: unit (did the answer come out in the requested unit?), sign/quadrant (is the direction or component plausible?), and magnitude (does the size make physical sense?). These three catch the overwhelming majority of avoidable errors.

Treat each randomized template below as a timed drill. Re-run it until you can reach the correct value, in the correct unit, in under two minutes without notes. That fluency — not memorized trivia — is what passes the FS.

content.text: Final checklist before you submit any answer: (1) degree mode on; (2) right formula form; (3) correct sign/quadrant; (4) unit converted (ft↔m, ft²↔acres, ft³↔yd³); (5) magnitude sanity-checked against an estimate.

content.title: Key Takeaways
items: Apply the five-step routine: unknown/unit → formula → signs/quadrant/mode → estimate → compute/convert.
Drill the randomized templates (Pythagorean, bearing↔azimuth, lat/dep, misclosure, area, EDM/temp, curve) to fluency.
Self-check every answer for unit, sign/quadrant, and magnitude.
Budget ~2.9 min/question; speed plus the checklist beats memorized trivia.

flashcards.definition: Identify the unknown and its unit → choose the formula → check signs/quadrant/angle mode → estimate the magnitude → compute and convert. The estimate step catches most blunders.
About 2.9 minutes per question (~110 questions in ~5h20m). Bank time on routine computations to fund harder conceptual items and to allow rechecking.
Before accepting any answer, verify the unit (is it what was asked?), the sign/quadrant (is the direction plausible?), and the magnitude (does the size make physical sense?).

quiz_questions.question_text: During the NCEES Fundamentals of Surveying (FS) exam, which reference may a candidate use?
options: Only the searchable NCEES FS Reference Handbook provided on-screen
The candidate's own printed formula sheet
quiz_questions.correct_answer: Only the searchable NCEES FS Reference Handbook provided on-screen
quiz_questions.explanation: The FS is closed-book except for the on-screen, searchable NCEES FS Reference Handbook. Personal textbooks or notes are not permitted, but it is not a no-reference exam.

quiz_questions.question_text: Passing the FS exam, together with the education requirement, qualifies a Texas candidate for which credential?
quiz_questions.explanation: The FS is the entry exam leading to the SIT. The RPLS requires further experience plus the PS and Texas exams; the LSLS is an additional state-land credential.

quiz_questions.question_text: In the inverse computation between two coordinate points, the azimuth is best computed as:
quiz_questions.explanation: Azimuth is measured clockwise from north, so it is atan2(easting difference, northing difference). The two-argument arctangent resolves the quadrant; reduce a negative result by adding 360°.

quiz_questions.question_text: Convert the bearing N 35°30' E to its decimal-degree azimuth.
quiz_questions.explanation: For a northeast bearing, the azimuth equals the bearing angle directly: 35°30' = 35.5°.

quiz_questions.question_text: How many square feet are in one acre?
quiz_questions.explanation: One acre equals 43,560 square feet (equivalently 10 square chains).

quiz_questions.question_text: A steel tape that is consistently 0.02 ft too long produces which kind of error?
options: No error, because it is constant
quiz_questions.explanation: A constant, one-directional effect from a mis-standardized tape is a systematic error; it can be modeled and corrected. Random errors vary in sign; blunders are gross human mistakes.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which statement correctly distinguishes accuracy from precision?
options: Accuracy is repeatability; precision is closeness to the true value
Accuracy is closeness to the true value; precision is repeatability of measurements
Precision improves only by removing systematic error
quiz_questions.correct_answer: Accuracy is closeness to the true value; precision is repeatability of measurements
quiz_questions.explanation: Accuracy reflects bias (closeness to truth); precision reflects scatter (agreement of repeated measurements). Averaging improves the mean's precision but cannot remove a systematic bias.

quiz_questions.question_text: Two independent distances are measured with standard errors of 0.03 ft and 0.04 ft. The standard error of their sum is:
quiz_questions.explanation: Errors of a sum combine in quadrature: sqrt(0.03² + 0.04²) = sqrt(0.0009 + 0.0016) = sqrt(0.0025) = 0.05 ft.

quiz_questions.question_text: A total station measures a slope distance of 500.00 ft at a zenith angle of 90°. The horizontal distance is:
quiz_questions.explanation: For a zenith angle, H = S·sin z. At z = 90° (a level sight), sin 90° = 1, so H = 500.00 ft.

quiz_questions.question_text: Convert the quadrant bearing S 20° E to an azimuth (degrees).
quiz_questions.explanation: For a southeast bearing, azimuth = 180° − bearing angle = 180° − 20° = 160°.

quiz_questions.question_text: The interior angles of a closed five-sided (pentagon) traverse should sum to:
quiz_questions.explanation: Sum of interior angles = (n − 2)·180° = (5 − 2)·180° = 540°.

quiz_questions.question_text: The sag correction for an unsupported steel tape is always:
options: Positive (added), because sag lengthens the tape
Negative (subtracted), because the sagging tape reads too long
Positive at low tension and negative at high tension
quiz_questions.correct_answer: Negative (subtracted), because the sagging tape reads too long
quiz_questions.explanation: A tape supported only at its ends hangs in a catenary, so the distance read along the tape exceeds the chord. The correction is always negative.

quiz_questions.question_text: In the height-of-instrument method, a backsight reading is:
options: Subtracted from the known elevation to get HI
Added to the known elevation to get the height of instrument (HI)
Always taken on a point of unknown elevation
quiz_questions.correct_answer: Added to the known elevation to get the height of instrument (HI)
quiz_questions.explanation: A backsight is a plus sight read on a point of known elevation: HI = known elevation + BS. Foresights are subtracted to obtain new elevations.

quiz_questions.question_text: A benchmark of elevation 412.50 ft is sighted with a backsight of 5.42 ft; a foresight of 7.10 ft is then read on a turning point. The TP elevation is (ft):
Making backsight and foresight distances equal at each setup primarily cancels which errors?
quiz_questions.explanation: Balancing sight distances makes these distance-dependent systematic errors affect both the backsight and foresight equally, so they cancel in the elevation difference.

quiz_questions.question_text: Given a GNSS ellipsoid height h and a geoid height N, the orthometric height H is:
quiz_questions.explanation: Orthometric height equals ellipsoid height minus the geoid height: H = h − N. In the conterminous U.S., N is negative, so H comes out larger than h.

quiz_questions.question_text: The Texas State Plane Coordinate System uses which projection and how many zones?
quiz_questions.explanation: Because Texas is wider east-west than north-south, its SPCS uses the Lambert Conformal Conic projection, divided into five zones: North, North Central, Central, South Central, and South.

quiz_questions.question_text: To obtain ground distance from a State Plane grid distance, you should:
options: Multiply the grid distance by the combined factor
Divide the grid distance by the combined factor
Multiply by the grid scale factor only
quiz_questions.correct_answer: Divide the grid distance by the combined factor
quiz_questions.explanation: Grid distance = ground distance × combined factor, so ground distance = grid distance ÷ combined factor (the product of grid scale factor and elevation factor).

quiz_questions.question_text: A traverse course runs S 50° W. The signs of its latitude and departure are:
quiz_questions.explanation: South gives a negative latitude and west gives a negative departure, so both components are negative in the SW quadrant.

quiz_questions.question_text: A closed traverse with perimeter 3,000 ft has a linear misclosure of 0.15 ft. Its relative precision is approximately 1:N, where N is:
quiz_questions.explanation: Relative precision = perimeter ÷ misclosure = 3,000 ÷ 0.15 = 20,000, i.e., 1:20,000.

quiz_questions.question_text: The compass (Bowditch) rule distributes traverse misclosure to each course in proportion to:
options: The latitude and departure magnitudes of the course
The interior angle at the course's start
quiz_questions.explanation: The compass rule corrects each course in proportion to its length. Distributing in proportion to latitude/departure magnitude is the transit rule.

quiz_questions.question_text: A rectangular parcel measures 300 ft by 200 ft. Its area in acres is (to two decimals):
Simpson's one-third rule for area requires:
options: An even number of intervals (odd number of offsets)
quiz_questions.correct_answer: An even number of intervals (odd number of offsets)
quiz_questions.explanation: Simpson's one-third rule fits parabolas across pairs of intervals, so it needs an even number of equal intervals (an odd number of offsets). A leftover odd interval is handled by the trapezoidal rule.

quiz_questions.question_text: Two cross-sections of areas 120 ft² and 160 ft² are 50 ft apart. By average end area, the volume in cubic yards is:
Why does a GNSS receiver require a minimum of four satellites for a 3-D position?
options: To average out multipath at four angles
Three for position plus one to solve the receiver-clock bias
One satellite per constellation is required
Four are needed only for RTK, not static
quiz_questions.correct_answer: Three for position plus one to solve the receiver-clock bias
quiz_questions.explanation: There are four unknowns — three position coordinates and the receiver clock offset — so four pseudorange equations (satellites) are the minimum.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which GNSS observable is required to achieve centimeter-level surveying accuracy?
quiz_questions.explanation: Code positioning is meter-level. Centimeter accuracy requires carrier-phase observations with the integer ambiguity resolved (a 'fixed' solution).

quiz_questions.question_text: Regarding dilution of precision (DOP), a lower DOP value indicates better positioning geometry.
quiz_questions.explanation: DOP measures how satellite geometry amplifies ranging error into position error; a lower DOP means well-distributed satellites and a better (more accurate) position.

quiz_questions.question_text: A simple horizontal curve has R = 600 ft and intersection angle I = 40°. Compute the tangent length T (ft).
quiz_questions.explanation: T = R·tan(I/2) = 600 × tan(20°) = 600 × 0.36397 = 218.38 ft. Remember to halve I before taking the tangent.

quiz_questions.question_text: A right triangle has legs of 90 ft and 120 ft. The hypotenuse is (ft):
quiz_questions.explanation: c = sqrt(90² + 120²) = sqrt(8,100 + 14,400) = sqrt(22,500) = 150 ft (a 3-4-5 triangle scaled by 30).

problem_templates.name: Right-triangle hypotenuse (Pythagorean theorem)
problem_templates.question_template: A right triangle has legs measuring {{a:f2}} ft and {{b:f2}} ft. Using the Pythagorean theorem, what is the length of the hypotenuse, in feet?

solution_steps_template.title: Add and take the square root

problem_templates.explanation_template: By the Pythagorean theorem, c = sqrt(a^2 + b^2) = sqrt({{a:f2}}^2 + {{b:f2}}^2) = {{_answer}} ft. The hypotenuse is always longer than either leg.

problem_templates.name: Convert a SE quadrant bearing to azimuth
problem_templates.question_template: A line has the quadrant bearing S {{b:dms}} E (south-and-east). Compute the AZIMUTH of the line (clockwise from north), in decimal degrees.

solution_steps_template.title: Identify the quadrant
solution_steps_template.calculation_template: S {{b:f2}} E -> SE quadrant (azimuth 90 to 180 deg)

problem_templates.explanation_template: A southeast bearing converts to azimuth with azimuth = 180 - bearing angle = 180 - {{b:f2}} = {{_answer}} deg.

problem_templates.name: Latitude of a traverse course
problem_templates.question_template: A traverse course runs N {{ang:f0}} deg E for a horizontal distance of {{dist:f2}} ft. Compute the LATITUDE (north-south component) of this course, in feet.
problem_templates.explanation_template: Latitude = d cos(alpha) = {{dist:f2}} x cos({{ang:f0}} deg) = {{_answer}} ft north. (Its departure, d sin(alpha), would be {{dep:f2}} ft east.)

problem_templates.name: Linear misclosure of a closed traverse
problem_templates.question_template: After summing all courses of a closed-loop traverse, the latitudes total {{sumLat:f3}} ft and the departures total {{sumDep:f3}} ft. Compute the LINEAR MISCLOSURE, in feet.
problem_templates.explanation_template: Linear misclosure is the hypotenuse of the latitude and departure misclosures: E = hypot({{sumLat:f3}}, {{sumDep:f3}}) = {{_answer}} ft.

problem_templates.name: Parcel area by coordinates (shoelace), in acres
problem_templates.question_template: A closed four-sided parcel has corner coordinates (X, Y), in feet, of A(0, 0), B({{xb}}, 0), C({{xb}}, {{yc}}), and D(0, {{yd}}), taken counter-clockwise. Using the coordinate (shoelace) method, what is the parcel area in ACRES? (1 acre = 43,560 sq ft.)

solution_steps_template.title: Apply the shoelace sum
solution_steps_template.calculation_template: Cross-multiply A(0,0), B({{xb}},0), C({{xb}},{{yc}}), D(0,{{yd}}) around the loop

problem_templates.explanation_template: The shoelace method yields a double area of {{xb}}*{{yc}} + {{xb}}*{{yd}}; halving gives {{areaSqFt:f1}} sq ft, which is {{areaSqFt:f1}} / 43,560 = {{_answer}} acres.

images_needed.caption: Quadrilateral A,B,C,D with coordinates labeled for the shoelace computation

problem_templates.name: EDM/steel-tape temperature correction
problem_templates.question_template: A distance of {{L:f2}} ft is taped with a steel tape standardized at 68 degF. The field temperature is {{T:f1}} degF. Using alpha = 0.00000645 per degF, compute the TEMPERATURE CORRECTION (in feet) to be applied to the measured distance (give the signed value).
problem_templates.explanation_template: Ct = alpha (T - T0) L = 0.00000645 x ({{T:f1}} - 68) x {{L:f2}} = 0.00000645 x {{dT:f1}} x {{L:f2}} = {{_answer}} ft. A positive value means the tape was hotter than standard (longer), so the correction is added.

problem_templates.name: Tangent length of a simple horizontal curve
problem_templates.question_template: A simple horizontal curve has a radius R = {{R}} ft and an intersection (central) angle I = {{I}} degrees. What is the tangent length T (ft) from the PC to the PI?
problem_templates.explanation_template: The tangent distance from the PC to the PI of a simple curve is T = R*tan(I/2) = {{R}} x tan({{I}} deg / 2) = {{_answer}} ft. Remember to halve I before taking the tangent.

images_needed.caption: Simple curve with tangent T from PC to PI for radius R and central angle I
